message:
refactor(frankenterm-core): extract storage health/repair surface into storage/health.rs (ft-fxymo / ft-dn2tu Phase 3)

Continues the storage.rs slim-down (parent bead ft-dn2tu Phase 3, sub-
bead ft-fxymo) by lifting the database health-check, stats, and repair
functions plus their report types out of the 30k-line storage.rs into
a sibling module. Mechanical move with public-API preservation; no
behavior change.

Module wiring (crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs):

- New `pub mod health;` declaration sibling to the existing `pub mod
  mmap_store;`.
- `pub use health::{ DbCheckItem, DbCheckReport, DbCheckStatus,
  DbRepairItem, DbRepairReport, DbStatsReport, EventTypeStats,
  PaneStats, TableStats, check_database_health, database_stats,
  repair_database };` so existing call sites
  (`ft doctor`, `ft db stats`, `ft db repair`, proptest_storage)
  continue to import these symbols from
  `frankenterm_core::storage::*` unchanged.
- WAL_RECOVERY_THRESHOLD is promoted from private to `pub(crate)`
  with a comment explaining why: storage::health::check_database_health
  reuses the same threshold to gate the WAL-frame health check, and
  duplicating the constant would invite drift between the recovery
  path and the health-check path. Ownership stays in storage.rs
  (used by check_and_recover_wal); health.rs reaches for it via the
  crate-internal visibility.

New file (crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage/health.rs, 777 lines):

- Header docstring records the bead id (ft-fxymo / ft-dn2tu Phase 3),
  the public-API preservation contract, the call sites that depend on
  it, and the planned next phase (ft-6qkx1, which will pull the Db*
  report types into a future storage/types.rs).
- Carries the verbatim function bodies of database_stats,
  check_database_health, and repair_database plus the
  DbStatsReport / TableStats / PaneStats / EventTypeStats /
  DbCheckReport / DbCheckItem / DbCheckStatus / DbRepairReport /
  DbRepairItem types. No logic edits.

Beads sync (.beads/issues.jsonl): captures the in_progress / closed
transitions for the affected sub-beads alongside this code commit so
the JSONL stays consistent with the workflow events.

Net effect on storage.rs is a small grow (a `pub mod` line + 10 re-
exports + a 4-line comment block + the visibility bump on
WAL_RECOVERY_THRESHOLD) accompanied by removal of the corresponding
function/type bodies, with their full text now living one directory
deeper. End state matches the Phase 1a test extraction's pattern:
storage.rs as a thin schema/migration coordinator, sibling modules
under storage/ owning the rest.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -85,10 +85,12 @@
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2. Both RobotCommands::Workflow::Run (main.rs:19831) and ::Abort (main.rs:20520) now build PolicyEngine with config.safety.require_prompt_active and chain .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules — matching the construction at RobotCommands::Send (main.rs:17652) and non-robot workflow sites (21361/21533/25051/27347). Regression test robot_workflow_policy_engine_denies_send_when_prompt_required_ft_4z7vh asserts SendText is denied when require_prompt_active=true + command_running. Test binary compile is currently blocked by pre-existing concurrent-agent breakage (E0603 macro select private, E0063 missing defer_fts_triggers field) unrelated to this fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-5490a","title":"[golden-artifacts] TOON encoder: format=toon emits across robot+MCP, no exact-bytes corpus pinning the rendered output","description":"## The gap

ft's robot mode and MCP layer support `format=toon` for token-
efficient AI-to-AI output (per README's TL;DR: 'TOON-compact —
use this when piping to an AI'). Two existing tests:

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (21 KB) — proves
  `serialize → deserialize == identity` for arbitrary inputs.
- `tests/wa_mission_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` and
  `wa_tx_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` — assert TOON output for specific
  mission/tx envelopes parses back into the expected JSON shape.
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` — pins ONE TOON output (pane
  state). 5.6 KB.

Property tests enforce shape invariants. The mission/tx tests
enforce parses-back-to-JSON. **Neither pins the exact rendered
TOON bytes for the broader robot envelope corpus.**

`grep -lE "toon.*golden\|TOON_GOLDEN\|to_toon.*golden"
tests/*.rs` returns zero hits beyond `golden_pane_state_toon.rs`.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

The README's TL;DR sells TOON specifically as 'token-efficient AI-
to-AI output'. The encoded *bytes* are the value proposition — a
new field on a robot envelope that adds 50 tokens per response,
or a re-ordering that makes the output less compressible by an
AI's vocabulary, breaks the entire 'token-efficient' contract
silently. Property tests don't catch this because they preserve
roundtripability, not byte-economy.

Concrete drift hazards a corpus would catch:
- TOON encoder version change reorders fields, blowing up token
  counts.
- A robot tool gains an `Option<String>` field that serializes as
  `key=null,` even when None — invisible in JSON, present in TOON,
  uses tokens.
- Whitespace drift between major TOON versions silently hits the
  full robot/MCP surface.

## Suggested implementation

Mirror the structure of `tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (which I
shipped runtime-validation for in ft-5ikbd) but for TOON output:

1. **Corpus** at `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.toon`
   with one frozen TOON rendering per robot tool that pins
   meaningful output (state, get-text, search, send, mission_state,
   tx_run, tx_show, …).
2. **Generator** (or test-side fixture builder) that produces a
   deterministic input envelope per fixture and renders it via the
   production TOON encoder. The same input is checked into
   `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.input.json` so the
   regeneration command is auditable.
3. **Test driver**
   `tests/conformance_toon_envelope_corpus.rs`:
   - For each `<tool>.input.json`, render via the production TOON
     encoder, scrub timestamps/elapsed_ms/durations, diff against
     `<tool>.toon`.
   - Bonus: assert byte-count is within ±10% of a stored
     `<tool>.byte_budget.txt` so regressions in token-economy
     surface as test failures (not just silent bloat).
4. **UPDATE_GOLDENS workflow** — same pattern as the existing
   `tests/golden/` infrastructure (PROVENANCE.md row, diff review).

## Out of scope

- Comparing TOON to JSON byte counts (token economy is the focus,
  but cross-format comparison is a separate analysis).
- Reranking / weighting of which tools are highest-value to pin
  (start with the README-enumerated robot family).

## Acceptance criteria

- ≥10 `<tool>.toon` goldens across the robot envelope family.
- A new field on a robot tool's response struct that serializes
  as TOON makes the corresponding golden fail with a precise diff.
- Byte-budget assertion (or equivalent) flags >10% regression.
- PROVENANCE.md row records the encoder version pinned at
  generation.

## References

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (existing property coverage)
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` (the existing single TOON
  golden — template for the corpus expansion)
- README §TL;DR (TOON's stated value proposition)
- ft-5ikbd (sibling: JSON envelope schema validator that established
  the corpus directory pattern)

Filed by cc_1 golden-artifacts audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cod_4","created_at":"2026-04-28T09:27:38.550302Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:57:22.407021Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T09:57:22.406860Z","close_reason":"added exact-byte TOON robot/MCP goldens and conformance test","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-55l4f","title":"[review][MEDIUM] wa_wait_for.json golden freezes polls:1 — may flake under high-concurrency CI load","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/wa_wait_for.json (landed in ecae6caa):

    "polls": 1

The canonicalize fn in crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs only zeroes `now | elapsed_ms | captured_at | *_ms | score | semantic_score`. The `polls` field is not canonicalized.

Under load, this can flake:
- Each wa.wait_for poll spawns a fresh python3 subprocess (fake-wezterm.py) via tokio::Command in crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:2142-2155.
- On first-poll cache-cold with the swarm running 20+ agents hammering CPU/disk, the subprocess spawn + file read can be slow enough that the poll scheduler issues a second poll before the first returns matching text (build ready matches ready$ immediately on content).
- polls jumps to 2+, golden is polls==1, test fails.

Repro likelihood: low under bench, moderate-to-high under concurrent cargo test + rch + other agent builds hammering I/O.

Fix options (pick one):
1. Canonicalize `polls` to 0 in canonicalize() alongside elapsed_ms/now/captured_at. Pro: stable golden. Con: loses a genuine signal — golden no longer catches regressions where wait_for suddenly needs 10 polls for a match it used to catch on the first try.
2. Split `polls` out of the golden capture: keep it in success_envelope but canonicalize to "polls >= 1" assertion in assert_wait_for_success_data (already there at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:~590 — `polls >= 1`), then canonicalize polls to 0 so the golden only sees the structural presence.
3. Accept the flake risk and document that re-running tests clears transient failures.

Option 2 is cleanest: the semantic "polls >= 1" invariant stays enforced; the byte-stable golden covers structure.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a correctness bug; CI flake risk. Discovered during reviewer audit round 7, 2026-04-23 when verifying ecae6caa (ft-qyw1z+ft-4o5mb CRITICAL fix).

Tag: review, mcp, conformance, flake.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:43:30.203619Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843645Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:47:17.843379Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 05c8d36b (Option 2 from the bead). Semantic invariant polls>=1 remains enforced in assert_wait_for_success_data at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:474-477 (runs against the live envelope at :733, BEFORE canonicalization). canonicalize() at :480 now zeroes polls alongside now/elapsed_ms/captured_at so the byte-stable golden only freezes structural presence, not scheduler-timing-dependent count. wa_wait_for.json updated to polls:0 to match. Only golden in the tree carrying a polls key is wa_wait_for.json, so the canonicalize rule is unambiguous and cannot mask a real regression in another tool's envelope.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}

Equal merge keys can therefore produce different merged sequences across processes/platforms.  {"id":"ft-58uc0","title":"[gap] README wait-for rule-fire claim not matched by crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs","description":"README.md:144 claims `ft robot wait-for` blocks until a specific rule fires; grep shows the command is defined as "Wait for a pattern in pane output" at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:2454-2473 and implemented via `wezterm::compile_wait_matcher` + `PaneWaiter` polling at crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:17782-17864.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:08.856774Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T04:19:01.722606Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:14.644794Z","close_reason":"done — README said 'blocks until a specific rule fires' but implementation waits for pattern match in pane output; fixed wording (9f9a7c40)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs"]}
 {"id":"ft-5cl1b","title":"[codebase-audit][fleet/replay] cross-architecture replay determinism unverified — README markets 'deterministic replay' across heterogeneous fleets","description":"## Domain gap

The README §Replay & Forensics section markets:

> Capture, replay, and diff decision graphs for post-incident
> analysis and regression testing.

The "Decision replay | Normalized DAG + causal edges + diff
engine" claim in the Key Algorithms table implies replay
across hosts. The `tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs` (26 KB)
covers same-host determinism. But concrete **cross-architecture
determinism** (e.g., capture on aarch64-apple-darwin, replay on
x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) is unverified.

## What's missing (not caught by prior audits)

Sources of cross-architecture nondeterminism that none of the
prior audits surfaced:

1. **Floating-point ordering.** Any `Vec<f64>::sort_by` or
   reduction that's not bit-identical across compiler/CPU
   variants. The `recorder_query.rs` ranking, the
   `hybrid_search.rs` RRF fusion, the `bocpd.rs` change-point
   detector — all candidates.

2. **HashMap iteration order.** Default `HashMap` iteration
   order depends on RandomState seed → SipHash with an arch-
   specific entropy pool. Replay events are PRESERVED in
   serialized form, but **derived structures** (HashMap-backed
   indexes, `extra` field iteration) diverge across hosts.

3. **`time::Instant` resolution.** macOS uses
   `mach_continuous_time()` (1ns); Linux uses `CLOCK_MONOTONIC`
   (typically 1µs). Replay timestamps may quantize differently;
   any duration arithmetic that depends on tick boundaries
   diverges.

4. **`std::process::id()`, `std::thread::current().id()`.**
   Embedded in error messages, telemetry samples, replay
   captures. Tests pin a workspace-id but live process IDs
   leak through.

5. **Unicode width tables.** `unicode-width` crate version
   pinning is per-Cargo.lock, but the library's Unicode data
   table version may differ if bundled separately.

6. **`zstd` compression nondeterminism.** `byte_compression.rs`
   compresses pane output. zstd is deterministic on a single
   version + level + dictionary, but version drift across
   hosts breaks byte-identical comparison of compressed
   payloads.

## Why this is a real defect

The README's "post-incident analysis" use case is precisely the
cross-host case: an incident happens on host A; the operator
exports the snapshot; replay runs on host B (the engineer's
workstation). If replay diverges, the engineer can't reproduce
the failure. The marketing claim is then false.

The skill's `codebase-audit` lens specifically catches "marketing
claim with no test gate" — every prior audit examined ft on a
single host with single configuration; the cross-host gradient
is structurally invisible from inside any single-host test.

## Suggested approach

1. **Differential CI matrix**:
   - Capture a synthetic 100-event session on macos-14.
   - Upload the capture file as an artifact.
   - On ubuntu-latest, download the artifact and run
     `ft session replay <file>`.
   - Diff: pane state at each step, decision graph nodes,
     emitted events. Assert byte-identical OR document the
     accepted divergence classes (timestamps, process IDs).

2. **Determinism contract document** at
   `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md`:
   - Enumerate accepted nondeterminism (timestamps, PIDs,
     hostnames).
   - Enumerate must-be-deterministic (event ordering, decision
     edges, payload bytes).
   - For each "accepted" item, name the canonical scrubber.

3. **Scrubber library** for replay artifacts:
   `crates/frankenterm-core-replay/src/scrubbers.rs` with one
   scrubber per nondeterminism source (UUID, timestamp,
   process_id, etc.). Mirrors the
   `testing-golden-artifacts` skill's scrubber pattern.

4. **Replace any floating-point hot paths with integer
   arithmetic where determinism matters** (RRF fusion,
   change-point detection). If float is unavoidable, document
   the bit-identity expectation per platform.

5. **Test**: `tests/cross_arch_replay_determinism.rs` that
   simulates two architectures (via different `RandomState`
   seeds and time-quantum mocks) and asserts replay yields
   identical post-scrubbing output.

## Out of scope

- The `byte_compression.rs` zstd version pinning (orthogonal
  — pin via Cargo.lock).
- The full GUI replay UX (wezterm-side determinism is its own
  audit).

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md` exists with the
  accepted/required-determinism matrix.
- A CI job captures on macos-14 and replays on ubuntu-latest;
  passes byte-identical OR scrubbed-equal.
- The scrubber library covers every accepted-nondeterminism
  source.
- Regression test exercises two-architecture-mock determinism.

## References

- README §Replay & Forensics ("Decision replay | Normalized DAG")
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs`
  (single-host determinism only)
- `docs/proposals/ft-j1qjt-replay-tier1-blocker.md` (related
  replay work)
- Skill: codebase-audit — "domain coverage matrix" (cross-arch
  is the one axis no single-host test exercises)
- Skill: testing-golden-artifacts §Pattern 5 (Canonicalized
  Golden) — direct precedent for the scrubber library

 {"id":"ft-5hp7p","title":"[config-types][test] extracted crate tests miss toml dev-dependency","description":"frankenterm-core-config-types inline tests use toml::to_string_pretty/from_str in tuning_config.rs, but the extracted crate manifest only declared serde. cargo test -p frankenterm-core-config-types failed with E0433 unresolved crate toml until the dev-dependency is restored.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:05.296081Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:40.437023Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T22:07:40.436733Z","close_reason":"Added the missing toml dev-dependency so cargo test -p frankenterm-core-config-types passes. Landed in 08e48279.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-5ikbd","title":"[conformance] robot-mode JSON envelopes: no runtime validator against docs/json-schema/","description":"## The gap

`docs/mcp-api-spec.md` states (verbatim):

> `data` MUST match the corresponding robot JSON schema under
> `docs/json-schema/`.

This is the central MUST clause of the MCP/robot surface. There is no
runtime test that enforces it.

## What exists today

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` validates that:
  - Each `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` is well-formed JSON
  - Each registry entry maps to a file on disk
  - Each file has the required JSON-Schema metadata fields
  - `docs_gen` is deterministic
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/*.json` pins
  individual envelope *examples* but does not validate envelopes
  against the schema — it compares serialized output to a hand-crafted
  fixture.

## What's missing

- No `jsonschema` (or equivalent) crate in `Cargo.toml` workspace deps.
  `rg "jsonschema = " Cargo.toml crates/*/Cargo.toml` returns nothing.
- No test that takes a real `RobotResponse` / MCP tool envelope, runs
  it through `serde_json::to_value`, and validates against the
  corresponding `wa-robot-*.json` schema with a real Draft validator.
- The `additional_properties_leak` test in `schema_golden.rs` only
  checks the schemas themselves; a Rust type that adds a field but
  forgets to update the schema would land green.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

A `Robot*Response` struct gains a new `Option<u64>` field. The struct
serializes fine. `golden_robot_envelope/wa_state.json` tested a
specific case that doesn't exercise the new field, so it stays green.
`docs/json-schema/wa-robot-state.json` does NOT have the new field.
**Downstream JSON consumers (TypeScript clients, MCP agents) break.**
A schema-conformance harness catches this on the same commit that
adds the field.

This is exactly the differential / golden-file pattern the conformance
skill (Pattern 3) prescribes for serialization formats with an
authoritative spec.

## Suggested implementation

1. Add `jsonschema = "0.18"` (or equivalent) to dev-dependencies.
2. New test file `tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs`:
   - For each `(endpoint, schema_file)` pair from `SchemaRegistry`:
     - Deserialize the corresponding `golden_robot_envelope/*.json`
       fixture into the typed `Robot*Response`.
     - Re-serialize via `serde_json::to_value`.
     - Validate the serialized `Value` against the schema.
   - For each top-level production envelope returned by mcp_helpers,
     run a synthetic happy-path producer + validator.
3. Bonus: a coverage-matrix table at the bottom of the test that
   prints `(schema, validations_run, validations_passing)` so we can
   read it in CI and check that every shipped schema is reached.

## Out of scope

- Generating schemas FROM Rust types (that's `api_schema::SchemaRegistry`'s
  existing job; this bead validates the OTHER direction).
- Validating non-robot/MCP JSON outputs (separate beads if needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test conformance_robot_envelope_schema`
  passes locally and in CI.
- Adding a field to a `Robot*Response` struct without updating the
  schema makes the test fail with a clear, fixture-tied error message.
- Test prints a coverage table: schemas exercised vs schemas in registry.

## References

- `docs/mcp-api-spec.md` (the MUST clause)
- `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` (the schemas)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` (existing well-formedness gate)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (existing example fixtures)

Filed by cc_1 conformance audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:35:59.648425Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:02:39.771953Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T09:02:39.771768Z","close_reason":"Runtime JSON-schema validator for robot envelopes shipped at commit 04911fff7 (test) on top of 246328aa (dev-dep). tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs compiles docs/json-schema/wa-robot-envelope.json as a Draft 2020-12 validator and runs every wa_*.json success_envelope through it (14 validated, 1 skipped — the TOON conformance fixture has no envelope). Three falsification gates (missing-required-field, malformed-error-code, ok-true-without-data) prove the validator is not a no-op. fixture_inventory_is_visible prints the coverage table to stdout. Verified on macOS: 5/5 tests pass in 0.10s after a 1.15s incremental build. The MCP spec's central MUST clause ('data MUST match the corresponding robot JSON schema') now has runtime enforcement; a Rust struct gaining a field without a schema update will fail CI on the same commit.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-5jqed","title":"[ft-5cl1b] replace replay DecisionEvent DefaultHasher hashes with stable canonical digest","description":"ft-5cl1b found DecisionEvent::new using std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher for definition/input/output hashes.  {"id":"ft-5m5xc","title":"scan_pipeline: enable_ansi_analysis config field is dead — never read, setting false has zero effect","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs:40-62 ScanPipelineConfig declares 'pub enable_ansi_analysis: bool' with Default = true, but no code in the crate ever reads this field. process() at :405 and process_chunk() at :443 unconditionally call scan_newlines_and_ansi* regardless of the flag. An operator configuring 'enable_ansi_analysis = false' in their config expecting to disable the ANSI byte counting gets no behavior change. This is a reality-gap defect: the documented config surface lies about what it controls. Fix: gate the ansi-scan call on self.config.enable_ansi_analysis; when false, skip the scan and return a ScanMetricsSummary with ansi_byte_count=0 and ansi_density=0 (preserving the newline_count + logical_lines numbers that don't depend on ANSI scanning). Default stays true so existing callers see no change. Regression test: set enable_ansi_analysis=false, feed input containing ESC bytes, assert ansi_byte_count==0 in returned ScanOutput.metrics.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:47:35.328044Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:50.053903Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:50.053447Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3a4dfcd8 (fix(scan_pipeline): honor enable_ansi_analysis flag in batch + chunked paths). Two regression tests pin batch (process) + chunked (process_chunk) contracts, including a sanity check that the default config still counts ANSI bytes.  {"id":"ft-5o6u5","title":"[security] Redactor leaves base64 secret suffixes unmasked","description":"Scope: redactor.rs secret handling.

Finding: GENERIC_API_KEY / GENERIC_TOKEN / GENERIC_SECRET only consume alnum/underscore/dash (and token dot) values, then Redactor::redact replaces only the regex match. Base64-style client secrets containing /, +, or = are partially redacted and leave trailing secret bytes visible.

Evidence: crates/frankenterm-core/src/redactor.rs:47, crates/frankenterm-core/src/redactor.rs:53, crates/frankenterm-core/src/redactor.rs:65, crates/frankenterm-core/src/redactor.rs:220.

Repro unit:
let r = Redactor::new();
let input = "client_secret=abcdEFGHijklMNOP/QRST+UVWX=YZ1234567890";
let out = r.redact(input);
assert!(!out.contains("QRST+UVWX=YZ1234567890"));

Currently the assertion fails because the output leaves the /QRST+UVWX=YZ1234567890 suffix.

Impact: robot/MCP/audit paths that rely on Redactor can persist or return the unredacted suffix of common OAuth/client/base64 secrets.

Fix: extend generic secret/token/key value patterns to consume base64-ish quoted or whitespace-delimited values, and add regression coverage for /, +, and = values.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"amber-fox","created_at":"2026-04-27T19:37:13.774888Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T19:45:03.413816Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T19:45:03.413576Z","close_reason":"Extended GENERIC_API_KEY/GENERIC_TOKEN/GENERIC_SECRET value charsets to include base64 bytes /+= so OAuth/client_secret values are fully redacted. 5 regression tests added in redactor::tests covering all three patterns + whitespace-boundary preservation + detect() span. All pass via rch (frankenterm-core --lib redactor::). Commit 652600f4.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-761tz","title":"[HIGH] disk_pressure NaN sample silently reports Green tier instead of failing closed","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller passes `DiskSample { usage_fraction: NaN, .. }` to `DiskPressureMonitor::update_with_sample`. Easy trigger: `available/total` when `total == 0` (e.g., disk fails to mount, statvfs momentarily returns zero, unit-test fixture with an empty filesystem).
2. `EwmaEstimator::update` calls `sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0)`. **`NaN.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN** — this is documented Rust behaviour but caller-hostile. The clamp does not sanitise the NaN.
3. `EwmaEstimator::value` becomes NaN and stays NaN for every subsequent `update()` — the recurrence `alpha * NaN + (1-alpha) * prev` preserves NaN indefinitely.
4. `PidController::update` receives `pid_error = NaN - target`. Its internal integral + derivative go NaN and the output is NaN. Permanent until `reset()`.
5. `classify_tier(NaN, thresholds)` compares `NaN >= thresholds.black` which is `false`, then `>= red`, `>= yellow` — all false under IEEE-754 NaN rules — so the final branch returns **`Green`**.
6. Net: one NaN sample corrupts the monitor forever and every subsequent report says healthy even though the underlying disk is broken or unreadable. Operators trusting the Green tier take no action.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer, not a crash or a visible error.
- Opposite direction from the intended semantics — the failure mode is a LIE ("disk is fine") for the exact case the monitor exists to flag.
- One bad sample is sticky: the monitor cannot self-recover.

## Fix sketch

1. `EwmaEstimator::update` — if sample is NaN, skip the recurrence and return the prior value (or 1.0 if uninitialised). Do not poison internal state.
2. `PidController::update` — if error is NaN, skip state update and return 0.0 (or last output). Same no-poison rule.
3. `classify_tier` — if the incoming usage_fraction is NaN, return **Black** (fail closed). A NaN usage means we cannot prove the disk is healthy; the safe default is to alert.
4. Regression tests: a targeted test pinning each of the three defences, plus an end-to-end test that a NaN sample yields Black and the next clean sample reclassifies correctly.

## Scope

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs` only. No cross-crate API changes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:39:31.506608Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.150284Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.149967Z","close_reason":"Landed in c5a02edb (fix(disk_pressure): fail closed on NaN in Ewma, PID, and classifier) + regression tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-76d9i","title":"[audit] storage/search: validate_fts_query doubles full-scan cost on lexical queries","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:33:26.455763Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T06:55:02.015811Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T06:55:02.015533Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 2099121d: validate_fts_query now uses SELECT 1 ... Empty-match-set semantics preserved via QueryReturnedNoRows → Ok promotion. 2 new regression tests pin empty-set validation and 4-shape happy path (simple, prefix*, AND, OR). All 14 existing fts_search_* tests pass against /tmp/ft-pane0-76d9i-target.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3610,"issue_id":"ft-76d9i","author":"jemanuel","text":"Perf/contention finding from 2026-04-23 storage audit: every lexical FTS search currently does a redundant preflight full scan before the real query. Evidence: validate_fts_query runs SELECT COUNT(*) FROM output_segments_fts WHERE output_segments_fts MATCH ?1 LIMIT 1 at storage.rs:15010-15016, and search_fts_with_snippets calls it unconditionally before preparing/executing the actual snippet/BM25 query at storage.rs:15042-15048. COUNT(*) with LIMIT 1 still has to enumerate all matches for broad FTS terms, so common queries pay for two MATCH walks and hold the read connection longer under load. Expected: syntax validation should be a cheap prepare/step of the real query shape or a SELECT rowid ... LIMIT 1 probe, not an aggregate count over the whole match set.","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:34:43Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-7axpo","title":"protocol_recovery deterministic jitter defeats thundering-herd protection","description":"# [HIGH] protocol_recovery: fake jitter

**Subsystem:** protocol_recovery / retry
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/protocol_recovery.rs (`RecoveryConfig::delay_for_attempt`)

## The hole

\`\`\`rust
let jitter_seed = ((attempt as f64 * 7.13).sin().abs()).mul_add(2.0, -1.0);
let jittered_ms = jitter_range.mul_add(jitter_seed, capped_ms).max(1.0);
\`\`\`

`sin(attempt * 7.13)` is fully deterministic. Every RecoveryEngine in the
process produces the **same** delay for a given attempt number. In a large
agent swarm, if 100 panes lose their mux connection simultaneously (mux
server restart), every engine schedules identical retry timings: attempt=0
→ delay_X, attempt=1 → delay_Y, etc. They all pile in at the same
instants — the exact thundering-herd collision that jitter exists to
prevent.

Pre-fix: `jitter_fraction: 0.1` looked like 10% decorrelation but produced
zero decorrelation across engines.

## Fix

Replaced the deterministic `sin()`-derived seed with
`rand::rng().random_range(-jitter_range..=jitter_range)` — same approach
already used in `retry.rs::RetryPolicy::delay_for_attempt`. Consistent with
how the other module in the same crate spells jitter.

Kept the `jitter_range > 0.0` fast-path so `jitter_fraction == 0.0` is
still bit-exact deterministic (tests rely on this).

## Caller impact

`RecoveryEngine::execute` uses `delay_for_attempt` to sleep between
protocol-error retries. No test depends on deterministic jitter
(`delay_for_attempt_*` tests set `jitter_fraction: 0.0`), so the fix is
drop-in.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:26.938808Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102548Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102198Z","close_reason":"Fix already shipped in fdd1e817; regression tests added in bf4a63b9 (delay_for_attempt_jitter_is_randomized_across_calls + delay_for_attempt_zero_jitter_is_deterministic).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol-recovery","reliability","retry"]}
 {"id":"ft-7bcox","title":"[LOW] FrameWriter Drop flush may abort on double-panic during unwind (7c8deb1d smell)","description":"Fresh-eyes review of 7c8deb1d: Drop impl does I/O via BufWriter::flush. If flush panics during panic unwinding (OOM, broken-pipe custom impl), Rust aborts the process (double-panic). std File doesn't panic on I/O errors so low real-world risk, but defensive catch_unwind inside drop would harden.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T21:29:18.017407Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T02:00:38.491092Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T02:00:38.490826Z","close_reason":"2bb39b2d (linter-committed from my edits) wraps FrameWriter::drop's flush call in std::panic::catch_unwind with AssertUnwindSafe so a flush panic during a panic-unwind doesn't trip Rust's panic-during-unwind abort rule. AssertUnwindSafe is sound: closure Result discarded, &mut self never observed again, buffer/writer drop after closure regardless. Two new regression tests at recording.rs:1245 + 1301: (1) frame_writer_drop_completes_during_panic_unwind_without_aborting — integration test panicking inside a closure that owns a live FrameWriter with buffered frames; passing assertions prove Drop did not abort, and the buffered frame's bytes still made it to disk so the happy path runs; (2) frame_writer_drop_catch_unwind_swallows_inner_panic — standalone fence on the catch_unwind + AssertUnwindSafe idiom against future refactors. 5 frame_writer tests pass via rch on /tmp/ft-cc6-target.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["recording","review","review-of-review"]}

 {"id":"ft-cbeyl","title":"[MEDIUM] mcp_manifest_golden db-gated tool whitelist silently drifts when tool renamed or removed","description":"In commit 1bde42b3 (ft-nnfwn: freeze no-db mcp manifest surface),
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_manifest_golden.rs:254-316 hard-codes
three whitelists of tools/resources/templates expected to be db-gated:

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [
    \"wa.accounts\",
    \"wa.events\",
    \"wa.reserve\",
    \"wa.release\",
    \"wa.search\",
    \"wa.send\",
    \"wa.workflow_run\",
] {
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

The assertion only checks absence from \`no_db_tools\`. If a tool is
renamed (e.g., \`wa.accounts\` → \`wa.list_accounts\`) or removed entirely,
the whitelist entry silently stays true (\`!no_db_tools.contains(\"wa.accounts\")\`
is trivially true when \`wa.accounts\` no longer exists in either manifest).

The same drift pattern applies to the resource whitelist
(lines ~285, \`wa://accounts\`, \`wa://events\`, \`wa://reservations\`) and
the template whitelist (lines ~295, \`wa://accounts/{service}\` etc.).

Impact: the no-db subset guard gives false confidence. A tool could
accidentally become always-on (or be removed from db-gated registration)
without the test catching it.

Fix: for each whitelist entry, assert \`full_tools.contains(entry)\`
BEFORE checking \`!no_db_tools.contains(entry)\`. That way, if the
whitelisted tool disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test
fails and forces whoever made the change to update the whitelist
deliberately.

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [...] {
    assert!(
        full_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"whitelist drift: {expected_absent_tool} no longer exists in db-backed manifest — update the test\"
    );
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

Severity rationale: medium — test-quality issue that masks regressions
in MCP tool surface categorization. Not a correctness bug in production
code.

Evidence: commit 1bde42b3 diff lines 262-316 of the new
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` test body.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:49.960054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915565Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915363Z","close_reason":"Added full_*.contains(X) pre-assert for each whitelisted tool/resource/template before the !no_db.contains(X) check. If a whitelisted identifier disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test now fails instead of passing trivially. Fix landed in 4d52f550. Verified 6/6 tests pass via rch exec.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","test-gap","testing"]}

Add stable ordinals or vector-valued matches and test duplicate same-key decisions with different outputs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T20:57:36.076708Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T20:57:36.076708Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
